audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel chapter 15, first-pass draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Absalom's image campaign, real judicial access failure, religious pretext, uninformed followers, David's flight, ten concubines left behind, foreign/exile troops and children, public grief, ark politics, priestly intelligence network, Hushai's dangerous assignment, and leader-centered valuation.

verses.restored: After this Absalom prepared for himself a chariot, horses, and fifty men running before him.

Absalom rose early and stood beside road of gate. Whenever any man with dispute came to king for judgment, Absalom called and said, From what city are you? He said, Your servant is from one of tribes of Israel.
Absalom said to him, See, your matters are good and right, but there is no one appointed by king to hear you.
Absalom said, Who will appoint me judge in land, so every man with dispute or judgment may come to me and I will make him right?
When a man came near to bow to him, he stretched out his hand, took hold of him, and kissed him.
Absalom did according to this manner to all Israel who came to king for judgment. Absalom stole/turned hearts of men of Israel.
At end of forty years/four years, Absalom said to king, Please let me go and complete my vow that I vowed to YHWH at Hebron.
Your servant vowed while I lived at Geshur in Aram, saying, If YHWH truly returns me to Jerusalem, I will serve YHWH.
King said to him, Go in peace. He arose and went to Hebron.
Absalom sent spies throughout all tribes of Israel, saying, When you hear sound of shofar, say, Absalom reigns at Hebron.
With Absalom went two hundred men from Jerusalem who were called. They went in their innocence and did not know anything.
Absalom sent for Ahithophel the Gilonite, counselor of David, from his city Giloh, while he offered sacrifices. Conspiracy became strong, and people with Absalom continually increased.
Messenger came to David, saying, Heart of men of Israel is after Absalom.

David said to all his servants with him in Jerusalem, Arise and let us flee, because there will be no escape for us from face of Absalom. Hurry to go, lest he quickly overtake us, drive evil upon us, and strike city by edge of sword.
Servants of king said to king, According to all my lord king chooses, look, your servants.
King went out, and all his household at his feet. King left ten women, concubines, to guard house.
King went out and all people at his feet, and they stood at last house/place far off.
All his servants crossed beside him: all Cherethites, all Pelethites, and all Gittites, six hundred men who came at his feet from Gath, crossed before king.
King said to Ittai the Gittite, Why do you also go with us? Return and stay with king, because you are a foreigner and also an exile from your place.
You came yesterday; shall I make you wander with us today while I go wherever I go? Return and return your brothers with you. Covenant-kindness and faithfulness be with you.
Ittai answered king and said, As YHWH lives and as my lord king lives, in place where my lord king is, whether for death or life, there your servant will be.
David said to Ittai, Go and cross. Ittai the Gittite crossed, and all his men and all little ones who were with him.
All land wept with a great voice. All people crossed, and king crossed wadi Kidron. All people crossed toward way of wilderness.
Look, Zadok also and all Levites with him carried ark of covenant of Divine. They set down ark of Divine, and Abiathar went up until all people finished crossing from city.
King said to Zadok, Return ark of Divine to city. If I find favor in eyes of YHWH, YHWH will return me and show me it and its dwelling.
If YHWH says thus, I have no delight in you, look, here I am; let YHWH do to me what is good in YHWH's eyes.
King said to Zadok the priest, Are you not a seer? Return to city in peace, and your two sons with you: Ahimaaz your son and Jonathan son of Abiathar.
See, I will wait at crossings/plains of wilderness until word comes from you to report to me.
Zadok and Abiathar returned ark of Divine to Jerusalem and stayed there.
David went up ascent of Olives, going and weeping, his head covered, walking barefoot. All people with him covered each his head, going up and weeping.
It was told David, saying, Ahithophel is among conspirators with Absalom. David said, Please make counsel of Ahithophel foolish, YHWH.
David came to summit where he bowed to Divine. Look, Hushai the Archite came to meet him, his robe torn and dust upon his head.
David said to him, If you cross with me, you will become a burden to me.
If you return to city and say to Absalom, I will be your servant, O king; I was servant of your father then, and now I am your servant—then you may frustrate counsel of Ahithophel for me.
Are Zadok and Abiathar the priests not there with you? Every word you hear from house of king you shall tell Zadok and Abiathar the priests.
Look, their two sons are there with them: Ahimaaz of Zadok and Jonathan of Abiathar. By their hand send to me every word you hear.
Hushai, companion of David, came to city, and Absalom came to Jerusalem.

translation_notes: Absalom exploits a genuine governance failure: petitioners cannot access a royal judge. Charismatic manipulation and legitimate grievance can coexist.
Physical pageantry, kisses, and promises of universal justice build a personality-centered campaign. Appearance of closeness is not accountable public service.
The 'forty years' reading is chronologically difficult; many witnesses/readings support four years. The number must remain flagged.
Two hundred invited men do not know the conspiracy. Presence in a political crowd does not establish informed consent or guilt.
David leaves ten concubines to guard the palace. They are exposed to predictable danger without recorded consent and later become targets of political sexual violence.
Ittai is a Gittite, foreigner, and exile. David gives him an exit, but his soldiers and 'little ones' still enter a dangerous civil-war migration.
The ark is sent back rather than used as a royal talisman. Yet Zadok, Abiathar, and their sons become an intelligence network, placing religious workers and young messengers at risk.
David calls Hushai a burden because of tactical need; age, disability, distress, or reduced mobility must not make a person inherently burdensome.
The chapter's weeping includes a whole displaced population, not merely a fallen ruler.
safety_note: Do not romanticize political charm, weaponize religious vows, blame uninformed followers, or make refugees prove loyalty through danger. Center the ten women abandoned in the palace, children crossing into exile, ordinary petitioners denied justice, and people turned into intelligence assets by competing rulers.
